result.question: Find the best available brand name for a software product — an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline that fans out web + academic searches and writes long-form research reports in which every claim is traced to a verifiable citation, with a built-in verifier that re-grounds each sentence to source evidence (core differentiator: faithfulness/rigor, "a research analyst that never makes up a fact"). Replace the current name "Polaris" (too generic, heavily trademarked) and avoid the whole star/compass/navigation/"north star" cliché family. Naming style is open: short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable; coined/abstract words or obscure real words / Greek-Latin roots meaning truth/insight are welcome. Exclude names already used in the AI-research space (Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm, Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research, Glean, Hebbia, Vespa). Deliver a ranked shortlist of 8-12 candidates, each with pronunciation + rationale, GitHub org availability, USPTO/EUIPO trademark risk (Nice class 9 & 42), .ai/.com domain availability, X/Twitter handle availability, and a GREEN/YELLOW/RED risk flag — then a top recommendation that is GREEN on all channels.

angles.label: broad/primary — naming inspiration for truth/faithfulness positioning
angles.query: brandable startup name generator truth veracity clarity insight Latin Greek roots two syllable coined word
angles.rationale: Surfaces raw candidate name material and root-word inspiration (verus, sooth, limpid, lucid, evident, proof) that ties directly to the product's faithfulness/rigor/illumination positioning themes.

angles.label: competitive landscape — names already taken in AI research space
angles.query: AI research assistant startups 2025 2026 automated literature review citation agent company names list
angles.rationale: Maps the crowded naming space so we can exclude near-duplicates of Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Hebbia, Glean, etc., and spot which semantic territories (truth, scholar, cite) are already claimed.

angles.label: trademark verification — USPTO/EUIPO class 9 & 42 software marks
angles.query: how to search USPTO TESS EUIPO live trademark software Nice class 9 42 clearance name availability
angles.rationale: Establishes the method and sources for checking trademark risk on each finalist, which is a required deliverable field and a hard GREEN/RED gate.

angles.label: domain + handle availability — .ai/.com and X/Twitter checking
angles.query: check .ai .com domain availability whois X Twitter handle taken tools for startup brand name
angles.rationale: Covers the domain-acquisition-feasibility and social-handle deliverable fields, including how to tell parked vs registered vs available and rough acquisition cost signals.

angles.label: naming strategy — what makes coined tech brand names ownable and strong
angles.query: coined abstract company name Vercel Cohere Anthropic why brandable ownable naming best practices pronounceable
angles.rationale: Grounds the ranking criteria — which naming patterns are most defensible/ownable, easiest to trademark, and most pronounceable — so the shortlist and top pick are principled, not arbitrary.

result.summary: The question is a hybrid of creative brand-name generation and rigorous multi-channel availability verification, so the angles split into (1) generating meaning-aligned name material, (2) mapping the competitive/exclusion landscape, and three verification channels — (3) trademark, (4) domain + social handles — plus (5) a naming-strategy angle to make the ranking principled. Together they cover both idea sourcing and the GREEN/YELLOW/RED clearance evidence the deliverable demands.

results.title: 9 Best AI Tools for Research in 2026 (Free & Paid)
results.snippet: Names the core crowded competitive set to exclude near-duplicates of: Paperguide, Perplexity, Semantic Scholar, Elicit, Consensus, Scite, SciSpace, plus ChatGPT/Claude for synthesis. Directly maps the taken-name territory in the deep-research/citation space.

results.title: 7 Best Literature Review AI Tools in 2026 (Free + Paid)
results.snippet: Adds more claimed brand names beyond the exclusion list: ResearchRabbit, Litmaps, Undermind, plus Paperguide's 'citation grounding' framing. Useful for spotting that 'research/paper/lit + noun' compound names and the 'cite/scite' semantic slot are already occupied.

results.title: 7 Best AI Research & Analysis Agents in 2026 — Agentic.ai
results.snippet: Focuses specifically on autonomous 'deep research' agents (the exact product category), likely surfacing agentic-branded competitors and confirming that 'deep research' and 'agent' descriptors are generic feature names rather than ownable brands.

results.title: 11 Best AI Tools for Scientific Literature Review in 2026
results.snippet: Broader 11-tool roundup that widens the map of claimed names in the scientific-review niche, helping ensure a candidate shortlist avoids collisions with mid-tier but active competitors.

results.title: 7 Best AI Research Assistants (2026): Tested and Reviewed — Editage
results.snippet: Editorial-services vendor's tested list reinforces which assistant brands have real market presence (NotebookLM, Consensus, Scite, Elicit), useful for confirming the 'scholar/research assistant' semantic territory is saturated.

results.title: Best Literature Review Tools in 2026: 15 AI-Powered & Traditional Options Compared
results.snippet: Largest catalog (15 options incl. traditional tools) for exhaustive near-duplicate checking; captures the long tail of taken names and the recurring 'truth/verify/citation-grounding' positioning language rivals already use.

results.title: Instant Domain Search — real-time domain availability + built-in WHOIS
results.snippet: Directly serves the deliverable's domain field: checks availability across 800+ TLDs (including .ai and .com) in real time as you type, with a built-in WHOIS lookup for ownership history and registrant info. Its guide also warns that .ai results are intentionally conservative — domains without DNS records are NOT marked available because .ai registrars can premium/reserve names outside public DNS/WHOIS, a key caveat for judging feasibility.

results.title: APIVoid Parked Domain Check — detect parked vs active domains
results.snippet: Directly addresses the 'how to tell parked vs registered vs available' angle. A parked domain is registered-but-not-in-use (placeholder/ads/for-sale page); this tool programmatically detects that state, letting you distinguish a name that is truly free from one that is owned and merely parked (often meaning acquisition cost, not open registration).

results.title: Who Owns a .ai Domain — WHOIS guide + acquisition feasibility
results.snippet: Explains .ai-specific WHOIS quirks critical to the .ai availability field: .ai WHOIS shows only a create date (no expiry), and .ai domains renew in 2-year intervals, so expiry must be inferred. Covers how to find the owner and pursue acquisition of a taken/parked .ai name — directly informing 'approximate acquisition feasibility.'

results.title: Namechk — one-shot username + domain availability across platforms
results.snippet: Checks a single candidate name against many domains and social usernames simultaneously, covering both the domain and X/Twitter-handle deliverable fields in one pass — useful for a first-pass sweep of the 8-12 shortlist before deeper per-name WHOIS/TESS verification.

results.title: BrandSnag Twitter (X) Handle Checker — @handle availability
results.snippet: Targeted specifically at the social-handle deliverable field: quickly checks whether @<name> is free on X/Twitter, the fastest way to test handle ideas for each finalist without manually probing x.com URLs.

results.title: NameCheckly — all-in-one brand, domain (incl. .ai) + social checker
results.snippet: All-in-one checker spanning business name, domains (explicitly including .ai and .com), and social handles (X/Twitter, GitHub, etc.). Convenient for simultaneously screening GitHub org, domain, and social-handle availability per candidate, though results should be confirmed with authoritative WHOIS/TESS before finalizing.

results.title: What is abstract naming? The pros and cons of abstract brand names (Fabrik Brands)
results.snippet: Directly grounds the ranking criteria: coined/abstract names are the most ownable and trademarkable because you invent a new word unlikely to collide with existing marks. Also flags the trade-off — no inherent meaning, so associations must be built through marketing. Best single source for WHY coined names (Vercel/Cohere/Anthropic-style) are defensible.

results.title: Why Vowels Matter in Brand Names (Dozro)
results.snippet: Phonetic-design principle backing the pronounceability requirement in the shortlist. Argues vowel placement drives memorability and pronounceability — the mechanism behind why short 2-3 syllable coined names read cleanly. Useful for scoring how 'sayable' each finalist is.

results.title: Making the right choice: what should I name my tech company? (Structure)
results.snippet: Branding-agency framework for tech naming that stresses global pronounceability and distinctiveness for category-creating products — relevant since the deep-research agent is defining a rigor/faithfulness niche. Reinforces choosing maximally distinctive coined marks over descriptive ones.

results.title: Ultimate Guide to Brand Naming: Create Memorable Names (Spellbrand)
results.snippet: Warns that hard-to-spell/pronounce names are the most damaging naming mistake — if people can't spell it after hearing it they can't find you, and can't recommend it by word of mouth. Practical screen for eliminating finalists that fail the 'say it, spell it, find it' test.

results.title: Why Abstract Names Are Overused and Underperform (NamingForce)
results.snippet: Counterpoint that keeps the criteria honest: abstract names carry no built-in meaning and can underperform without heavy brand investment. Supports favoring coined names that at least carry a phonetic hint of truth/insight (semi-abstract) over pure gibberish for a positioning-heavy product.

results.title: Abstract business names – do unique brand names really work? (Dotinum)
results.snippet: Reiterates the ownership advantage — inventing a word from scratch means you won't collide with existing titles and can usually trademark and protect it. Corroborates the trademark-defensibility rationale for prioritizing coined candidates in the shortlist.

results.title: Search our trademark database | USPTO (Trademark Search — the TESS replacement)
results.snippet: The authoritative, free US-side gate for every finalist. TESS was retired Nov 30, 2023 and replaced by the cloud-based Trademark Search at tmsearch.uspto.gov — old TESS URLs now redirect here. This is where you run the required Class 9 (software) and Class 42 (SaaS/software services) clearance for each candidate name and confirm whether any conflicting mark is LIVE vs dead.

results.title: A Crash Course in TESS II, the USPTO's New Trademark Search System — BOAG LAW
results.snippet: Practical how-to for the new USPTO system: Boolean operators (AND/OR/NOT), field tags, truncation, and proximity searching, plus the Search Builder. Directly enables the method needed to search a candidate name string, filter to LIVE marks, and restrict to Nice classes 9 and 42 — the exact query construction required to produce the GREEN/YELLOW/RED trademark verdict per name.

results.title: TESS Is Gone: How to Search Trademarks in 2026 | Signa Blog
results.snippet: Current (2026) walkthrough confirming TESS's retirement and how to search today. Notes that the USPTO system covers US filings only, so EU clearance needs a separate tool — the key caveat for the deliverable, which requires both USPTO and EUIPO checks. Points to WIPO Global Brand Database and EUIPO's TMview for multi-jurisdiction coverage.

results.title: International Trademark Classes — Corsearch
results.snippet: Explains the Nice Classification and confirms software products live in Class 9 (downloadable software) with Class 42 covering software-as-a-service. Establishes exactly which two classes to filter on when clearing an AI-software brand name, the load-bearing scoping decision for the trademark field of each candidate.

results.title: Searching the Trademark ID Manual | USPTO
results.snippet: The USPTO ID Manual lets you confirm the correct goods/services descriptions and class assignments (9 vs 42) for AI/deep-research software before searching, ensuring the clearance query targets the right Nice classes rather than missing conflicts filed under adjacent identifications.

results.title: WIPO Global Brand Database (broadest free multi-office search)
results.snippet: The broadest free trademark search tool — 70M+ records across 70+ national/regional offices, including EUIPO — enabling the EU-side and international leg of the required per-name clearance that the US-only tmsearch.uspto.gov cannot cover. Pair with EUIPO's TMview for EU-specific live-mark checks in classes 9 and 42.

results.title: Word Root: ver (truth) — Membean
results.snippet: Definitive source on the Latin root 'ver' meaning truth/true (verus, veritas, veracity, verify, verdict). This is the single most on-target well of raw name material for the product's faithfulness/veracity positioning — coinable stems like Verus, Verity, Verra, Verid, Sooth-adjacent forms all trace here.

results.title: Veritas — Etymology, Origin & Meaning (Etymonline)
results.snippet: Rigorous etymology of veritas ('truth,' the Roman goddess of truth) from verus. Grounds truth-themed coined names (Verita, Vero, Veritas variants) with a credible academic lineage that fits the 'never makes up a fact' claim. Etymonline is the authoritative etymology reference, not SEO filler.

results.title: Sooth — Etymology, Origin & Meaning (Etymonline)
results.snippet: Documents 'sooth' as Old English for truth, from PIE *sntyós meaning literally 'that which is.' A rare, short, ownable English root directly meaning truth — excellent brandable material (Sooth, Soothe, Sothis) distinct from the crowded Latin field. Note the 'soothsayer=fortune-teller' semantic drift as a risk to weigh.

results.title: Word Root: Luc/Lum (light) — Wordpandit
results.snippet: Covers Latin luc/lum (lux, lumen, lucere 'to shine'; PIE *leuk-) feeding lucid, illuminate, elucidate. Directly serves the illumination/clarity theme — source stems for coined names like Lucid, Lumen, Lucere, Elucid that map onto 'making every claim clear and grounded.'

results.title: Lucid — Etymology, Origin & Meaning (Etymonline)
results.snippet: Traces 'lucid' (clarity of thought/expression) to lucidus/lucere and PIE *leuk-. Reinforces the clarity/illumination angle with authoritative etymology; useful for validating whether a lucid-derived coinage reads as 'clear thinking' vs merely 'bright.'

results.title: Brand Naming in 2026: Why Generated Names Don't Stick — Jacob Tyler
results.snippet: Argues the 'blank slate' strategy behind Notion, Figma, Vercel, Perplexity, Anthropic, Mistral: ownable, distinctive names that don't describe the product. Frames why a coined/abstract root-word name beats a literal star/compass name, and why trademark-clean abstraction matters — directly informs candidate selection strategy over pure generator output.

claims.claim: Because abstract names are novel and previously unheard, they are more memorable and stick in customers' minds.

claims.claim: Abstract names are invented words with no inherent meaning, distinct from compound names formed by combining existing words.

claims.quote: Class 42 applies to software development services and AI research services rather than the software products themselves.

claims.claim: The word 'sooth' derives from Old English 'soð' meaning 'truth, reality, fact' and 'true, genuine, real; just, righteous' — directly supporting a truth/faithfulness brand positioning.
claims.quote: Old English soð meant "truth, reality, fact," as well as "true, genuine, real; just, righteous."

claims.claim: 'Sooth' traces to Proto-Indo-European *hes-ont- meaning 'being, existence' and thus 'real, true,' giving the truth-meaning a deep verifiable etymological root.
claims.quote: Ultimately, it traces to Proto-Indo-European *hes-ont-, meaning "being, existence," and thus "real, true."

claims.claim: 'Sooth' is archaic/obsolete in modern English (obsolete after mid-17c., revived only as an archaism by Scott in the early 19c.), which affects its brandability and ownability.
claims.quote: Sooth remained "in common use until mid-17c. then obsolete until revived as an archaism early 19c. by Scott," becoming archaic in modern English

claims.claim: 'Soothsayer' means one who speaks truth or prophecy, showing the root's association with truth-telling — a potential near-variant to check against navigation/oracle clichés.
claims.quote: **Soothsayer**: One who speaks truth or prophecy

claims.claim: The related word 'soothe' originally meant 'show to be true' before shifting by the 1690s to 'quiet, comfort,' documenting a semantic drift away from the truth meaning.
claims.quote: **Soothe**: Originally meant "show to be true," evolving by the 1690s to mean "quiet, comfort"

claims.claim: The Latin roots 'luc' and 'lum' both mean 'light', deriving from Latin 'lux' (light) and 'lumen' (lamp/light source), making them a viable naming source for the illumination/clarity positioning theme.
claims.quote: The roots "luc" and "lum" both signify **light**. They derive from Latin, specifically from "lux" (light) and "lumen" (lamp/light source).

claims.claim: The root 'luc' yields 'lucid', meaning clear and easily understood, and 'illuminate', meaning to light up or make clear/explain — directly mapping to the truth/clarity/insight positioning.
claims.quote: **Lucid** | Clear and easily understood ... **Illuminate** | To light up or make clear; to explain

claims.claim: These roots carry both a literal 'brightness' sense and a metaphorical sense of understanding and clarity, supporting brandable coinages that evoke insight and rigor.
claims.quote: these roots ... evolved to represent both physical brightness and metaphorical concepts of understanding and clarity

result.publishDate: unknown (content references October 2025 updates and CAT 2026 prep; no explicit publish date)

claims.claim: USPTO's TESS trademark search system was retired on November 30, 2023 and can no longer be used to search US trademarks, so the research task's instruction to 'search USPTO TESS' must be redirected to the successor system.
claims.quote: The TESS system was retired on November 30, 2023, after 23 years of operation.

claims.claim: US trademark searches (for verifying candidate brand names against live marks) must now be performed at the USPTO's cloud-based Trademark Search system at tmsearch.uspto.gov, which replaced TESS.
claims.quote: The USPTO replaced TESS with a cloud-based system called Trademark Search, accessible at tmsearch.uspto.gov.

claims.claim: Comprehensive trademark clearance for a name should use the USPTO Advanced/Expert search together with WIPO and TMview databases rather than a single source.
claims.quote: Use USPTO Advanced Search (Expert Mode), WIPO, and TMview

claims.claim: EU/international coverage for checking a brand name's conflicts is available via TMview, which covers over 75 million trademarks from EU member states, and the WIPO Global Brand Database, which covers over 70 million records from 73 national offices.
claims.quote: covers over 75 million trademarks from EU member states

claims.claim: Trademark searches can be filtered by Nice classes 9 and 42 (the software/AI goods and services classes relevant to this product), as shown in the article's API example.
claims.quote: nice_classes: [9, 42]

claims.claim: The USPTO retired the old TESS trademark search system on November 23 and replaced it with a new system (referred to as 'TESS II', official name TBD).
claims.quote: The old TESS system "went down" on November 23, with the new system launching immediately after.

claims.claim: In the new USPTO search system, field codes must precede the search term (e.g. CM:METALLICA) rather than follow it, and boolean operators AND/OR/NOT must be capitalized.
claims.quote: Field codes must precede search terms: `CM:METALLICA` (not `METALLICA[CM]`)... Operators (AND, OR, NOT) must be capitalized

claims.claim: The new system allows combining a mark search with a goods/services (class) filter, e.g. CM:METALLICA AND GS:(music or recording), which is the mechanism for scoping a search to relevant Nice classes such as 9 and 42.
claims.quote: Example: `CM:METALLICA AND GS:(music or recording)` combines mark searches with goods/services filtering

claims.claim: A USPTO TESS search only covers filed trademark applications and registrations and is not a substitute for a comprehensive clearance search covering common-law (unregistered) trademark use.
claims.quote: The tool "can only provide information on trademark applications and registrations" and is explicitly not a substitute for comprehensive clearance searches that account for common law trademark use.

claims.claim: "Veritas" is a Latin word meaning literally 'truth, truthfulness, that which is true', directly supporting a truth/faithfulness brand positioning.
claims.quote: veritas is "literally 'truth, truthfulness, that which is true.'"

claims.claim: Veritas derives from Latin 'verus' ('true'), from PIE root *were-o- ('true, trustworthy'), which is the source of English words like verify, veracity, verdict, and verity — indicating a rich family of related brandable/coinable roots (e.g. Verus, Verity, Verify).
claims.quote: It derives from the Latin word *verus* meaning "true," which traces back to the Proto-Indo-European root *were-o- meaning "true, trustworthy."

claims.claim: The were-o- root underlies numerous English words including aver, veracious, veracity, verdict, veridical, verify, verisimilitude, verism, verity, and very — giving alternative naming candidates in the truth/rigor family.
claims.quote: the *were-o- root "might form all or part of" numerous English words including: aver, veracious, veracity, verdict, veridical, verify, verisimilitude, verism, verity, and very.

claims.claim: The phrase 'in vino veritas' entered English by the 1590s, meaning 'in wine, truth' — evidence that 'veritas' already has broad cultural/idiomatic recognition in English, which is a branding consideration (recognizability but also potential genericness).
claims.quote: The famous Latin phrase *in vino veritas* (appearing in English by the 1590s) translates to "in wine, truth"

claims.claim: The Latin root 'ver' means 'truth' or 'true', making it a semantically direct root for naming a product positioned on faithfulness/veracity.
claims.quote: The Latin root word ver means “truth” or “true.”

claims.claim: The 'ver' root generates a family of English truth-words usable as naming inspiration, including 'verify', 'veracity', 'verity', 'verdict', 'veritable', 'veracious', 'verisimilitude', and 'aver'.
claims.quote: This root is the word origin of a fair number of English vocabulary words, including ver dict and ver acity.

claims.claim: 'Veracity' specifically denotes truthfulness, and 'verity' denotes the truth or reality of something -- both short, brandable coinage candidates tied to the rigor positioning.
claims.quote: veracity : ‘truthfulness’ ... verity : The ver ity of something is the truth or reality of it.

claims.claim: The 'ver' root yields cross-Romance-language truth words (Spanish 'verdad', French 'verite', Italian 'verita', Portuguese 'verdade'), offering coined/abstract name variants.
claims.quote: the Spanish word for “truth” is ver dad, French for “truth” is ver ite, the Italian is ver ita, and the Portuguese is ver dade

claims.claim: 'Verisimilitude' and 'veritable' carry meanings of authenticity and appearance of being real/true, aligning with a verifier that re-grounds claims to source evidence.
claims.quote: Verisimilitude is something's authenticity or appearance of being real or true. ... Something that is ver itable is authentic, actual, genuine, or without doubt.

result.evidence: Corroborated by multiple independent authoritative sources beyond Membean. Etymonline (Online Etymology Dictionary) and Wiktionary confirm Latin "verus" = "true/real," from PIE *were-o- "true, trustworthy." English descendants verify, veracity, verity, verisimilitude, aver, and verdict all derive from this root. The claim ("ver means truth/true, a semantically direct root for a faithfulness/veracity product") is standard, uncontested etymology — no source disputes or qualifies it. Source quality (etymonline + Wiktionary) matches the modest strength of the claim. This is not a marketing claim; it is settled linguistic fact and not time-sensitive.

The claim is corroborated by multiple independent authoritative sources, not just the etymonline secondary. Wiktionary lists "veritas" (from verus 'true' + -tas abstract-noun suffix) as meaning "truth, truthfulness." Merriam-Webster and Wikipedia both give "truth" and note Veritas as the Roman goddess/virtue of truth. Etymonline's own quote — "literally 'truth, truthfulness, that which is true'" — matches the claim verbatim. English descendants "verity" and "very" trace to it. This is elementary, uncontested dictionary-grade Latin etymology, so a secondary source is fully sufficient; there is no fast-moving or marketing dimension and no contradicting source exists. The word does directly denote truth/truthfulness, supporting the intended truth/faithfulness brand theme.

Multiple independent authoritative sources confirm the claim verbatim. Merriam-Webster, Wiktionary, Dictionary.com, and etymonline all define Latin "veritas" as "truth, truthfulness, that which is true," from Latin "verus" (true), from PIE root *were-o- ("true, trustworthy"); English "very" and "verity" derive from it. The supporting quote is an exact match to the claim with no overreach. This is an uncontroversial, well-established lexical/etymological fact directly supporting a truth/faithfulness positioning. No source disputes it; the only minor addendum (veritas is also a personified Roman goddess of truth) does not contradict the core meaning.

The claim is corroborated by multiple independent authoritative sources beyond the cited etymonline. Wiktionary: veritas is from verus ("true, real") + the abstract-noun suffix -tas, meaning "truth." Merriam-Webster, Collins English Dictionary, and Wikipedia all confirm veritas = "truth, truthfulness" (also personified as the Roman goddess of truth), tracing to PIE *were-o- "true, trustworthy." This is a settled, uncontroversial Latin etymology, not an extraordinary claim; the quote directly supports it. No credible source disputes or qualifies it. The brand-positioning inference (truth/faithfulness) follows trivially from the meaning.
result.counterSource: None found — Wiktionary, Merriam-Webster, Collins, and Wikipedia all agree with the claim

result.evidence: Multiple independent, high-quality sources corroborate the claim. Etymonline (the standard online etymology dictionary) and Wiktionary confirm the Latin adjective 'verus' means 'true' (from PIE *were-o- 'true, trustworthy') and is the direct root of veracity (via verax 'truthful' > veritas 'truthfulness'), verity, verify, veritas, verdict, veridical, and verisimilitude. The Membean quote ('the Latin root word ver means truth or true') accurately reflects this. This is a settled, uncontroversial linguistic fact, not a marketing or time-sensitive claim, and no source disputes it. The root is a semantically direct fit for a truth/faithfulness/veracity positioning.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found; etymonline.com and en.wiktionary.org independently confirm the claim.

result.evidence: Confirmed via instantdomainsearch.com. Dedicated per-TLD pages exist at /domain-extensions/{tld}, including https://instantdomainsearch.com/domain-extensions/ai ("Find Available .ai Domains – Compare Prices from 5 Registrars"). The named dedicated-page TLDs include both .ai and .com. The main search checks 800+ TLDs (the quote 'hundreds of extensions' is if anything an understatement). Both .ai and .com — the two TLDs the naming task requires — are supported, each with a dedicated page. Source is primary and current (2026 pages). No contradicting evidence found.

Claim is fully supported and corroborated. Primary source instantdomainsearch.com states "Real-time domain availability search with instant results as you type" and references availability checking across "800+ TLD extensions" — directly matching the claim of real-time, as-you-type checking across "hundreds of TLDs." Multiple independent third-party sources confirm the same behavior: NameStall lists it as an instant search tool, AI Tools Prime titles it "Real-time domain availability checker," and search results describe it checking "800+ extensions in under 25 milliseconds as you type" with "no clicking search and waiting." This is an ordinary, directly-observable functional claim about a well-known live tool (not an extraordinary claim), it is current (a continuously-updated live service), and the supporting quote genuinely backs the claim. No credible source disputes or qualifies it.

The claim is directly supported by the primary source. Instantdomainsearch.com's own tagline reads "Real-time domain availability search with instant results as you type," and its features list confirms checking across "hundreds of extensions" (specifically "800+ TLD extensions"). This is corroborated by independent search results describing the tool as checking availability across 800+ extensions in real time as you type, querying multiple registrars simultaneously. This is a verifiable, self-evident product-feature claim (not an extraordinary/benchmark claim requiring heightened sourcing), and no source contradicts or heavily qualifies it. Minor caveat: it derives from the vendor's own marketing tagline, but the feature is trivially verifiable by using the tool and is universally described the same way, so the source quality matches the claim's modest strength.

Claim is well-supported and confirmed by multiple credible/primary-grade sources beyond the cited Membean page. Etymonline, Wiktionary, and Merriam-Webster all confirm Latin 'verus' = "true/real" and 'veritas' = "truth," from PIE root *were-o- ("true, trustworthy"). English derivatives verify (confirm truth), veracity (truthfulness), verdict (jury's "truth"), verity, and verisimilitude all descend from this root and carry truth/accuracy meanings. This is basic, well-established etymology — not an extraordinary or fast-moving claim, and the quote directly supports the stated meaning. The naming rationale (ver = semantically direct root for a faithfulness/veracity-positioned product) is accurate and appropriately scoped. No contradicting evidence found; multiple independent sources agree.

The claim is directly supported and independently corroborated. Etymonline states Old English "soð" meant the noun "truth, justice, righteousness, rectitude; reality, a true situation, certainty" and the adjective "true, genuine, real; just, righteous" — matching the claim's "truth, reality, fact" and "true, genuine, real; just, righteous." Wiktionary independently confirms: OE sōþ "truth" (also "true, actual, real"), from Proto-Germanic *sanþaz, from PIE *h₁es- "to be" (i.e., "that which is / real"). Merriam-Webster and Dictionary.com concur. This is a standard, uncontroversial linguistic etymology (not an extraordinary claim), it does not go stale, it is not marketing/benchmark fluff, and no source disputes it. The truth/faithfulness positioning inference follows straightforwardly from the attested meanings. The only trivial nuance: etymonline's exact noun gloss is "truth, reality... certainty" rather than the literal string "truth, reality, fact," but "fact" is a faithful paraphrase of the sense and does not constitute an overreach.
result.counterSource: No credible counter-source found; Wiktionary, Merriam-Webster, and Dictionary.com all corroborate etymonline.

result.evidence: Primary source (instantdomainsearch.com) directly supports both parts of the claim. WebFetch of the homepage returned "Real-time domain availability search with instant results as you type" and "search across hundreds of extensions" / "800+ TLD extensions." This confirms (a) real-time checking as the user types and (b) coverage across hundreds of TLDs. The claim's "hundreds of TLDs" is accurate and, if anything, conservative versus the site's stated 800+. This is a self-verifiable functional product feature (not an extraordinary claim), sourced from the primary vendor site, with current 2026-dated supporting pages (e.g., "Domain Extensions List ... (2026)"). No contradicting or qualifying evidence found. Only minor nuance: the tool is a checker/comparison front-end aggregating registrar data, but that does not undercut the real-time-as-you-type or hundreds-of-TLDs claim.

Confirmed via primary source. instantdomainsearch.com supports 800+ TLDs including both .ai and .com (the two TLDs the naming task requires); the "hundreds of extensions" quote if anything understates coverage. Dedicated per-TLD pages are verified live: the dedicated .ai page at instantdomainsearch.com/domain-extensions/ai loads with .ai-specific search ("Search .ai domains"), 5-registrar pricing, and 2026 registry stats (990,913 registered .ai domains). Search results confirm the same /domain-extensions/{tld} pattern exists for .com and other extensions. This is a mundane operational claim about a domain-search tool, so primary-source verification is sufficient; data is current (2026) and no contradicting evidence exists.
